content:
Hi,

I'm new to Rust and I'm pretty sure I'm already in over my head. I am trying to implement DebugFS wrappers. I know that there have been previous attempts at this, including https://github.com/msm8227-mainline/linux/commits/msm8227-6.14-rust from @wctrl. However, I can't wait for other people to solve my problems for me, so I'm charging ahead regardless.

Here is what I have so far. Unfortunately, it doesn't compile. I need help fixing the compilation.

https://github.com/ttabi/linux/commits/debugfs/

Looking at @wctrl's code, it's obviously more complete but also seems very different from what I have. I don't know how to resolve these differences since there's a lot about his code that I don't understand. So what I'm trying to say is that I'd like to move forward with my implementation if that's okay, but I need a lot of help and I need to make progress in small changes.

The good news is that this is the only thing I'm working on, so I have the bandwidth to make improvements quickly.
I would suggest showing the errors you get and a description of the issue so that others can easily help.

The error is in the commit description, but I'll paste it here:

    error[E0606]: casting `&'a mut T` as `*mut c_void` is invalid
      --> rust/kernel/debugfs.rs:64:85
       |
    64 |             crate::bindings::debugfs_create_file_full(name.as_ptr(), mode, _parent, data as *mut ffi::c_void,
       |                                                                                     ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^

    error: aborting due to 1 previous error

    For more information about this error, try `rustc --explain E0606`.

The debugfs_create_file functions (there are a few of them) take one or two opaque "void *" parameters. These are supposed to point to some local struct and are passed back to the callback functions in the driver.

I'm having trouble implementing this in Rust.

Timur Tabi said:

The error is in the commit description, but I'll paste it here:

    error[E0606]: casting `&'a mut T` as `*mut c_void` is invalid
      --> rust/kernel/debugfs.rs:64:85
       |
    64 |             crate::bindings::debugfs_create_file_full(name.as_ptr(), mode, _parent, data as *mut ffi::c_void,
       |                                                                                     ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^

    error: aborting due to 1 previous error

    For more information about this error, try `rustc --explain E0606`.

seems like you're casting a reference to a raw pointer while changing the pointee type, that's not allowed, you'll need two casts, or better, use a coercion let data: *mut T = data;and then .cast::<c_void>().
That worked, thanks!

let data: *mut T = data;

Just for my understanding, does this change a reference to T into a pointer to T?
yes
https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/type-coercions.html#r-coerce.types.mut-to-pointer

OK, so i took a quick look at your code
it looks like benno already fixed the compile error.
i have a couple of notes:
1) it looks like some of your abstractions are unsound
debugfs_create_file_full takes a &'a mut T as an argument but the returned dentry stores &mut T so if anyone tries to read the data after T is dropped bad things will happen.
you could either add a associated lifetime to struct DebugfsEntry or (what i would recommend) require that T impl ForeignOwnable and get the data through into_foreign / from_foreign

2) debugfs_create_file_full will always fail to set the file operations
because you pass a reference to a stack allocated fops: bindings::file_operations, as soon as it returns, the fops is dropped. so the pointer stored in the dentry points to invalid memory

also if you are planning to upstream this, public public api's should wherever possible avoid using bindings. I would recommend taking a look at the #[vtable] macro and the miscdevice api https://rust.docs.kernel.org/src/kernel/miscdevice.rs.html because they already build a vtable based on file_operations.

Ok, so I don't know how to fix (1) or (2), so I'm just going to try to figure it out and come up with something that's probably just broken in some other way.
But I don't understand that last paragraph. The whole point behind this exercise is to come up with a Rust API to make debugfs calls that allows Rust drivers to avoid making unsafe calls directly. How am I supposed to do that without using bindings?
"the returned dentry stores &mut T" What do you mean by "returned dentry"? Do you mean DebugfsEntry.dentry, because that's a *mut crate::bindings::dentry.
Hmm.. I just noticed Opaque<T> in types.rs. I think this is what 'data' and 'aux' are supposed to be.

Hmm, I'm not getting anywhere with Opaque, even though it makes sense to use it. I can define data as an Opaque<ffi::c_void>, but I can't seem to extract the pointer from that type in order to pass it onto bindings::debugfs_create_file_full sigh

I think I understand what I'm doing wrong. Opaque<T> is intended to make a structure opaque, not a pointer to it. It still feels like I'm missing something. How do you define a pointer to a generic struct in Rust, like "void *" is in C?
you can use *const ()
and yes, Opaque<T> is used to make T opaque

So what's better:

pub fn debugfs_create_file_full<'a, T, U>(name: &CStr, mode: bindings::umode_t,
        parent: Option<DebugfsEntry>,
        data: &'a mut T, aux: &'a U,
        fops: &bindings::file_operations) -> Result<Self>

or

    pub fn debugfs_create_file_full<'a, T, U>(name: &CStr, mode: bindings::umode_t,
        parent: Option<DebugfsEntry>,
        data: * const(), aux: * const (),
        fops: &bindings::file_operations) -> Result<Self>

The latter is simpler, but it feels like I'm just abandoning any hope of preserving lifetimes.
I'm not familiar with debugfs, what is this API for? Is this something a driver would call?
what does the 'a lifetime represent? and what are T and U supposed to do/represent?
Yes, it's an API for creating and managing entries in /sys/kernel/debug. It's used mostly to expose debug info to user-space.

T and U are supposed to be driver-specific data. It's the usual callback mechanism: driver allocates some specific data in a struct, passes a pointer to that data to the debugfs registration function, and later when the kernel calls some callback function in the driver, it passes the pointer back to the callback function.
It's a very common paradigm in the kernel. I can't seem to find an example of something similar that I can mimic, however.

Timur Tabi said:

Yes, it's an API for creating and managing entries in /sys/kernel/debug. It's used mostly to expose debug info to user-space.

in the case of a driver API, we generally avoid raw pointers and types from bindings::, there are some exceptions, but they need to be well-justified
Well, at some point, the Rust driver needs to call debugfs_xxx to create the debugfs entries. You can't do it without calling these functions.

Timur Tabi said:

T and U are supposed to be driver-specific data. It's the usual callback mechanism: driver allocates some specific data in a struct, passes a pointer to that data to the debugfs registration function, and later when the kernel calls some callback function in the driver, it passes the pointer back to the callback function.

ah yeah in that case a mutable reference is the wrong way to go about things, you need ownership of the data, since the kernel can call the callback at any time after registration

Timur Tabi said:

It's a very common paradigm in the kernel. I can't seem to find an example of something similar that I can mimic, however.

yeah, I'm pretty sure we already do have this pattern everywhere, currently trying to find one that also uses generic data
Thank you. I know I'm in over my head. I just need some hand-holding until I can get over the initial hump.
so I'm not sure if this is exactly what you're looking for, but rust/kernel/miscdevice.rs might help you a bit
in your current design, how does the caller specify the callback?
that should most likely be handled by some trait (like the MiscDevice one)
In the fops struct. It's basically a struct of function pointers. I think #[vtable] is applicable, but I don't know how.
I'm familiar with miscdevice.rs. I'll take another look.
yeah so the fops struct should go away and be replaced by a trait with #[vtable]
What does #[vtable] actually do?

for data and aux (I don't know why you have two different types there, maybe one suffices), they should be generics implementing ForeignOwnable, that way you can convert them to and from raw pointers under the hood
ok, that's a lot to digest.

Timur Tabi said:

What does #[vtable] actually do?

for every function foo that you have in the body of the trait, it gives you access to a bool constant HAS_FOO that is true when the implementer of the trait has specified the method (for this to work, you need to specify a default implementation in the trait definition). it allows you to set it to NULL when it isn't defined, as that AFAIK makes the C side select the default implementation
that way we don't have to reimplement the default behavior on the rust side

Timur Tabi said:

ok, that's a lot to digest.

I can imagine, if you have any questions feel free to post them here, I might not answer right away, but maybe someone else will :)
thanks

So I'm looking at miscdevice.rs and trying to use that as a basic. It's still incredibly complicated. Part of the problem is that a miscdevice is an actual type of driver, and so it needs to be "registered" and has various callbacks. DebugFS, on the other hand, is just about the callbacks. I'm going to try to figure how to extract just the callback parts from rust_misc_device.
yeah, I don't know about the other parts of debugfs, but you should be able to just start off with a trait copying MiscDevice

Most of what I see in MiscDevice doesn't apply. For debugfs, there is no equivalent to bindings::miscdevice. For a miscdevice driver, the driver fills in a bindings::miscdevice struct, and then just calls misc_register with a pointer to that struct. But for debugfs, there is no "struct debugfs". All calls to debugfs just pass in parameters.
So I already something that does that, it's just that it seems to be wrong, but I just don't know how to fix it. https://github.com/ttabi/linux/commit/be4a248f42f72140a0fcdbe9570293e6d7950b90

What do you mean by this:

yeah so the fops struct should go away and be replaced by a trait with #[vtable]

What exactly does that look like?

Do you mean something like this:

#[vtable]
pub trait FileOperations: Sized {

because there's already a "pub struct file_operations" in bindings_generated.rs, that looks like this:

#[repr(C)]
#[derive(Copy, Clone)]
pub struct file_operations {
    pub owner: *mut module,
    pub fop_flags: fop_flags_t,
    pub llseek: ::core::option::Option<
        unsafe extern "C" fn(arg1: *mut file, arg2: loff_t, arg3: ffi::c_int) -> loff_t,
    >,

Shouldn't this have a #[vtable] on top?
It feels weird defining a file_operations struct in the debugfs.rs file, because file_operations is not from debugfs. It's from linux/fs.h, so it's part of the normal file system code.

I updated my repo: https://github.com/ttabi/linux/commits/debugfs/
It compiles, surprisingly, but there's a lot wrong with it still.
I believe Matthew has a basically finished debugfs implementation that he intends to send soon.
Do you have a link? Matthew who?

Timur Tabi said:

Do you have a link? Matthew who?

[PATCH 0/8] rust: DebugFS Bindings
